CLAIM: Supporting evidence
EVIDENCE: Consequentialists have distinguished three components of their theory: (1) their thesis about what makes acts morally wrong, (2) their thesis about the procedure agents should use to make their moral decisions, and (3) their thesis about the conditions under which moral sanctions such as blame, guilt, and praise are appropriate. What we might call full rule-consequentialism consists of rule-consequentialist criteria for all three. Thus, full rule-consequentialism claims that an act is morally wrong if and only if it is forbidden by rules justified by their consequences. It also claims that agents should do their moral decision-making in terms of rules justified by their consequences. And it claims that the conditions under which moral sanctions should be applied are determined by rules justified by their consequences. Full rule-consequentialists may think that there is really only one set of rules about these three different subject matters. Or they may think that there are different sets that in some sense correspond to or complement one another. Much more important than the distinction between different kinds of full rule-consequentialism is the distinction between full rule-consequentialism and partial rule-consequentialism. Partial rule-consequentialism might take many forms. Let us focus on the most common form. The most common form of partial rule-consequentialism claims that agents should make their moral decisions about what to do by reference to rules justified by their consequences, but does not claim that moral wrongness is determined by rules justified by their consequences. Partial rule-consequentialists typically subscribe to the theory that moral wrongness is determined directly in terms of the consequences of the act. This theory of wrongness is called act-consequentialism. Distinguishing between full and partial rule-consequentialism clarifies the contrast between act-consequentialism and rule-consequentialism. Act-consequentialism is best conceived of as maintaining merely the following: Act-consequentialist criterion of wrongness: An act is wrong if and only if it results in less good than would have resulted from some available alternative act. When confronted with that criterion of moral wrongness, many people naturally assume that the way to decide what to do is to apply the criterion, i.e., Act-consequentialist moral decision procedure: On each occasion, an agent should decide what to do by calculating which act would produce the most good. However, consequentialists nearly never defend this act-consequentialist decision procedure as a general and typical way of making moral decisions (Mill 1861: ch 2; Sidgwick 1907: 405–6, 413, 489–90; Moore 1903: 162–4; Smart 1956: 346; 1973: 43, 71; Bales 1971: 257–65; Hare 1981; Parfit 1984: 24–9, 31–43; Railton 1984: 140–6, 152–3; Brink 1989: 216–7, 256–62, 274–6; Pettit and Brennan 1986; Pettit 1991, 1994, 1997: 156–61; de Lazari-Radek and Singer 2014: ch. 10). There are a number of compelling consequentialist reasons why the act-consequentialist decision procedure would be counter-productive. First, very often the agent does not have detailed information about what the consequences would be of various acts. Second, obtaining such information would often involve greater costs than are at stake in the decision to be made. Third, even if the agent had the information needed to make calculations, the agent might make mistakes in the calculations. (This is especially likely when the agent’s natural biases intrude, or when the calculations are complex, or when they have to be made in a hurry.) Fourth, there are what we might call expectation effects. Imagine a society in which people know that others are naturally biased towards themselves and towards their loved ones but are trying to make their every moral decision by calculating overall good. In such a society, each person might well fear that others will go around breaking promises, stealing, lying, and even assaulting whenever they convinced themselves that such acts would produce the greatest overall good. In such a society, people would not feel they could trust one another. This fourth consideration is more controversial than the first three. For example, Hodgson 1967, Hospers 1972, and Harsanyi 1982 argue that trust would break down. Singer 1972 and Lewis 1972 argue that it would not. Nevertheless, most philosophers accept that, for all four of the reasons above, using an act-consequentialist decision procedure would not maximize the good. Hence even philosophers who espouse the act-consequentialist criterion of moral wrongness reject the act-consequentialist moral decision procedure. In its place, they typically advocate the following: Rule-consequentialist decision procedure: At least normally, agents should decide what to do by applying rules whose acceptance will produce the best consequences, rules such as “Don’t harm innocent others”, “Don’t steal or vandalize others’ property”, “Don’t break your promises”, “Don’t lie”, “Pay special attention to the needs of your family and friends”, “Do good for others generally”. Since act-consequentialists about the criterion of wrongness typically accept this decision procedure, act-consequentialists are in fact partial rule-consequentialists. Often, what writers refer to as indirect consequentialism is this combination of act-consequentialism about wrongness and rule-consequentialism about the appropriate decision procedure.
STRENGTH: moderate

And it claims that the conditions under which moral sanctions should be applied are determined by rules justified by their consequences. Full rule-consequentialists may think that there is really only one set of rules about these three different subject matters. Or they may think that there are different sets that in some sense correspond to or complement one another. Much more important than the distinction between different kinds of full rule-consequentialism is the distinction between full rule-consequentialism and partial rule-consequentialism. Partial rule-consequentialism might take many forms. Let us focus on the most common form. The most common form of partial rule-consequentialism claims that agents should make their moral decisions about what to do by reference to rules justified by their consequences, but does not claim that moral wrongness is determined by rules justified by their consequences. Partial rule-consequentialists typically subscribe to the theory that moral wrongness is determined directly in terms of the consequences of the act. This theory of wrongness is called act-consequentialism. Distinguishing between full and partial rule-consequentialism clarifies the contrast between act-consequentialism and rule-consequentialism. Act-consequentialism is best conceived of as maintaining merely the following: Act-consequentialist criterion of wrongness: An act is wrong if and only if it results in less good than would have resulted from some available alternative act. When confronted with that criterion of moral wrongness, many people naturally assume that the way to decide what to do is to apply the criterion, i.e., Act-consequentialist moral decision procedure: On each occasion, an agent should decide what to do by calculating which act would produce the most good. However, consequentialists nearly never defend this act-consequentialist decision procedure as a general and typical way of making moral decisions (Mill 1861: ch 2; Sidgwick 1907: 405–6, 413, 489–90; Moore 1903: 162–4; Smart 1956: 346; 1973: 43, 71; Bales 1971: 257–65; Hare 1981; Parfit 1984: 24–9, 31–43; Railton 1984: 140–6, 152–3; Brink 1989: 216–7, 256–62, 274–6; Pettit and Brennan 1986; Pettit 1991, 1994, 1997: 156–61; de Lazari-Radek and Singer 2014: ch. 10). There are a number of compelling consequentialist reasons why the act-consequentialist decision procedure would be counter-productive. First, very often the agent does not have detailed information about what the consequences would be of various acts. Second, obtaining such information would often involve greater costs than are at stake in the decision to be made. Third, even if the agent had the information needed to make calculations, the agent might make mistakes in the calculations. (This is especially likely when the agent’s natural biases intrude, or when the calculations are complex, or when they have to be made in a hurry.) Fourth, there are what we might call expectation effects. Imagine a society in which people know that others are naturally biased towards themselves and towards their loved ones but are trying to make their every moral decision by calculating overall good. In such a society, each person might well fear that others will go around breaking promises, stealing, lying, and even assaulting whenever they convinced themselves that such acts would produce the greatest overall good. In such a society, people would not feel they could trust one another. This fourth consideration is more controversial than the first three. For example, Hodgson 1967, Hospers 1972, and Harsanyi 1982 argue that trust would break down. Singer 1972 and Lewis 1972 argue that it would not. Nevertheless, most philosophers accept that, for all four of the reasons above, using an act-consequentialist decision procedure would not maximize the good. Hence even philosophers who espouse the act-consequentialist criterion of moral wrongness reject the act-consequentialist moral decision procedure. In its place, they typically advocate the following: Rule-consequentialist decision procedure: At least normally, agents should decide what to do by applying rules whose acceptance will produce the best consequences, rules such as “Don’t harm innocent others”, “Don’t steal or vandalize others’ property”, “Don’t break your promises”, “Don’t lie”, “Pay special attention to the needs of your family and friends”, “Do good for others generally”. Since act-consequentialists about the criterion of wrongness typically accept this decision procedure, act-consequentialists are in fact partial rule-consequentialists. Often, what writers refer to as indirect consequentialism is this combination of act-consequentialism about wrongness and rule-consequentialism about the appropriate decision procedure.
STRENGTH: moderate

[[ ## reasoning ## ]]
This is the first speech of the AC, so I need to establish a clear, reasonable interpretation of the resolution that is both philosophically grounded and accessible to a community college judge. The skeleton provides a strong framework: the resolution is valid when interpreted as "consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating means by outcomes" rather than "any outcome justifies any action." I'll use the Hooker 16 evidence to establish the philosophical distinction between act-consequentialism (judging wrongness by consequences) and rule-consequentialism (using rules as decision procedures). The word budget is 800 words, so I need to be concise but impactful, using clear language and real-world examples that resonate with a community college judge. I'll structure the speech to first diagnose the debate around reasonable interpretation, then establish the philosophical framework, then provide historical evidence, and finally resolve the tension between the two positions.

[[ ## speech_text ## ]]
Thank you. The resolution before us is simple: The ends can justify the means. But what this resolution actually means is where the debate will be decided. I affirm this resolution under the only reasonable interpretation: Consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating whether particular means are justified by their particular outcomes in specific circumstances. This is not the same as saying any outcome justifies any action. This is the only way to understand the resolution in ordinary language, and this is the only way to understand consequentialist ethics correctly.

Let me diagnose the actual disagreement. The Negative will claim I am defending extremism. They will say I am arguing that we can justify any action if the outcome is good. But that is not what the resolution says, and that is not what I am arguing. The resolution says the ends can justify the means. The word can implies possibility, not universality. It asks whether there are any circumstances where a good outcome can be a sufficient reason to adopt a difficult action. This is a claim about how moral evaluation works, not a command to do whatever works.

To understand why this interpretation is correct, we must understand consequentialist ethics. According to philosopher Mark Hurley's distinction, consequentialist ethics has three components: what makes acts morally wrong, what procedure agents should use to make moral decisions, and what conditions justify moral sanctions. The key point is this: act-consequentialism judges wrongness by consequences. As Hurley explains, "An act is wrong if and only if it results in less good than would have resulted from some available alternative act."

This matters because it establishes the core principle: means are justified if they produce net-positive outcomes. But here is where we resolve the tension between extremism and restraint. Consequentialist ethics does not require us to calculate every outcome in every situation. In fact, as Hurley explains, consequentialists reject the act-consequentialist decision procedure because "very often the agent does not have detailed information about what the consequences would be of various acts." Instead, we use rules whose acceptance will produce the best consequences.

This is the reasonable middle ground. We evaluate means by their outcomes, but we do so through practical rules that account for human limitations. This is not a license for tyranny. This is the framework that allows us to make moral decisions in the real world.

Let me illustrate with two historical cases. First, the Manhattan Project. The development and use of nuclear weapons ended World War II faster, saving millions of lives. The means were controversial, even horrific. But the outcome was net-positive. Under consequentialist ethics, this outcome justifies the means. Not because we should casually embrace nuclear weapons, but because this case demonstrates that evaluating means by outcomes is the only ethical framework that adapts to new evidence and changing circumstances.

Second, smallpox eradication. This required mandatory vaccination programs and quarantine measures that would be condemned under deontological ethics. But the outcome was the complete eradication of a disease that killed millions. The means were justified by the outcome. Again, this is not an endorsement of unlimited government power. It is an example of how weighing outcomes over fixed rules saves lives.

Now let me address the tension directly. The Negative will cite cases like human sacrifice or slavery to claim my position is extremist. But this misreads both the resolution and my argument. The resolution asks whether the ends can justify the means. It does not ask whether every end justifies every means. The Negative's examples prove my point: we condemn slavery not because of some fixed rule, but because of the net-negative outcomes it produces. Consequentialist ethics demands we weigh all foreseeable harms and benefits.

This is the key insight: means are justified by outcomes, not by any outcome. If an outcome is net-negative, the means are not justified. This resolves the moral tension without endorsing extremism.

So let me be clear about what I am affirming and what I am not. I affirm that consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating means by outcomes. I do not affirm that any outcome justifies any action. The resolution is true under the only reasonable interpretation, and this interpretation is both philosophically sound and practically necessary for moral decision-making.

This matters because it provides the only ethical framework that adapts to new evidence and changing circumstances. Fixed rules cannot account for the complexity of human life. Only outcome evaluation can guide us through moral uncertainty.

For these reasons, I urge an affirmative ballot. The resolution is true when understood as consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating means by outcomes. This is the only way to read the resolution in ordinary language, and this is the only way to understand consequentialist ethics correctly.

But that is not what the resolution says, and that is not what I am arguing. The resolution says the ends can justify the means. The word can implies possibility, not universality. It asks whether there are any circumstances where a good outcome can be a sufficient reason to adopt a difficult action. This is a claim about how moral evaluation works, not a command to do whatever works.

To understand why this interpretation is correct, we must understand consequentialist ethics. According to philosopher Mark Hurley's distinction, consequentialist ethics has three components: what makes acts morally wrong, what procedure agents should use to make moral decisions, and what conditions justify moral sanctions. The key point is this: act-consequentialism judges wrongness by consequences. As Hurley explains, "An act is wrong if and only if it results in less good than would have resulted from some available alternative act."

This matters because it establishes the core principle: means are justified if they produce net-positive outcomes. But here is where we resolve the tension between extremism and restraint. Consequentialist ethics does not require us to calculate every outcome in every situation. In fact, as Hurley explains, consequentialists reject the act-consequentialist decision procedure because "very often the agent does not have detailed information about what the consequences would be of various acts." Instead, we use rules whose acceptance will produce the best consequences.

This is the reasonable middle ground. We evaluate means by their outcomes, but we do so through practical rules that account for human limitations. This is not a license for tyranny. This is the framework that allows us to make moral decisions in the real world.

Let me illustrate with two historical cases. First, the Manhattan Project. The development and use of nuclear weapons ended World War II faster, saving millions of lives. This is the key insight: means are justified by outcomes, not by any outcome. If an outcome is net-negative, the means are not justified. This resolves the moral tension without endorsing extremism.

So let me be clear about what I am affirming and what I am not. I affirm that consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating means by outcomes. I do not affirm that any outcome justifies any action. The resolution is true under the only reasonable interpretation, and this interpretation is both philosophically sound and practically necessary for moral decision-making.

This matters because it provides the only ethical framework that adapts to new evidence and changing circumstances. Fixed rules cannot account for the complexity of human life. Only outcome evaluation can guide us through moral uncertainty.

For these reasons, I urge an affirmative ballot. The resolution is true when understood as consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating means by outcomes. This is the only way to read the resolution in ordinary language, and this is the only way to understand consequentialist ethics correctly.

We are NEG (opposing the resolution) in an AC-CX (Affirmative Cross-Examination) period. The Affirmative (AC) has just given their first speech, and we are cross-examining them.

The AC's speech:
- They interpret the resolution as: "Consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating whether particular means are justified by their particular outcomes in specific circumstances."
- They stress that the resolution uses "can" (possibility, not universality) and that they are not advocating for "any outcome justifying any action."
- They use Hurley's distinction to argue that consequentialism judges wrongness by consequences, but they reject act-consequentialist decision procedures in favor of rule-based approaches for practicality.
- They give examples: Manhattan Project (nuclear weapons) and smallpox eradication (mandatory vaccination) as cases where the means were justified by the outcomes.

Our perspective (NEG) is built on two main values, but the key belief we are going to attack is the one that says: "The ends can justify the means" leads to moral inconsistency and erosion of ethical governance (VALUE 1: Moral consistency requires that justifying harmful means undermines ethical governance).

We have available strategies: 
- expose_weakness
- establish_contradiction
- set_up_argument
- clarify_position
- challenge_evidence

Our goal in CX is to:
1. Expose weaknesses in the AC's argument (particularly their interpretation and examples).
2. Seek concessions that undermine their position (e.g., that they are actually advocating for a more extreme position than they claim, or that their examples don't hold up).
3. Set up traps for later speeches (e.g., get them to admit that in some cases they would justify harmful means, which we can then attack in the next speech).

Key points to target from the AC's speech:
- They say the resolution is about "possibility" (not universality). But they also say that consequentialism requires evaluating means by outcomes. However, they then give examples of harmful means (nuclear weapons, forced vaccination) that were justified by outcomes. But note: the Negative will argue that these examples are actually condemned in the long run (e.g., nuclear weapons led to arms races, forced vaccination might lead to distrust) or that the AC's interpretation is too broad.

But note: the AC claims they are not saying "any outcome justifies any action", but they are saying that for specific outcomes (like saving lives) the means are justified. We need to get them to concede that in the cases they cite, the means were not actually justified (or that they are inconsistent).

However, the AC also says: "If an outcome is net-negative, the means are not justified." So they are not claiming that all outcomes are net-positive. But we can challenge them on whether they can consistently draw the line.

Our strategy: We want to expose the weakness in their argument by challenging their examples and forcing them to concede that their interpretation leads to the very extremism they claim to avoid.

Specifically, we can target:
- The Manhattan Project: They say it saved lives. But we can ask: what about the long-term consequences (e.g., nuclear proliferation, the arms race, the fact that it led to the Cold War and the threat of mutual destruction)? Is the outcome really net-positive? If not, then the means (using nuclear weapons) were not justified under their own framework. But note: they might say that at the time, the outcome was net-positive (ending WWII quickly). So we need to get them to commit to a specific time frame.

- The smallpox eradication: They say mandatory vaccination was justified. But what if the mandatory vaccination led to a loss of public trust in health authorities, making future public health efforts harder? Is that a net-negative? They might say it was net-positive because the disease was eradicated. But we can ask: if the means (forced vaccination) led to a situation where people were so distrustful that they refused future vaccinations, then the outcome might be net-negative. So we need to get them to admit that they are relying on a specific outcome (eradication) and not considering long-term effects.

But note: the AC's speech does not explicitly say they consider long-term effects. They say "in specific circumstances", but we can challenge them on whether they are considering all foreseeable consequences.

Also, note the AC's claim: "Consequentialist ethics does not require us to calculate every outcome in every situation. Instead, we use rules whose acceptance will produce the best consequences." So they are saying we use rules (like "don't use nuclear weapons" as a rule) but in the Manhattan Project, they are saying the rule was broken because the outcome was so good. This is a contradiction: if we have a rule against nuclear weapons, then we shouldn't break it even for a good outcome? But they are saying the rule was broken because the outcome was so good. So they are saying the rule is not absolute.

We can challenge them on whether they are actually advocating for a rule that allows breaking rules for good outcomes, which would mean that the rule is not a rule at all (it's just a guideline that can be broken arbitrarily).

So, the core weakness: Their argument that they are not advocating for extremism is undermined because they are saying that in some cases (like the Manhattan Project) the means are justified even though they are harmful. And they are using the word "can" to say it's possible, which means they are admitting that there are cases where harmful means are justified. But then they say they are not advocating for "any outcome justifying any action", which is true, but they are admitting that in some cases (like the Manhattan Project) they would justify a harmful means. And the Negative's point is that once you admit that, you open the door to justifying any harmful means (if you can find a good outcome for it).

So, we want to get them to concede:
1. That in the Manhattan Project, the means (using nuclear weapons) were justified by the outcome (ending WWII and saving lives).
2. That they would consider a similar situation (e.g., using a nuclear weapon to end a current war) as justifiable, even though it's a harmful means.
3. But then we can say: if you justify nuclear weapons in one case, why not in another? And then we can bring up the example of human sacrifice (which they say they don't mean) but we can ask: if the outcome is to save a million lives by sacrificing one person, would that be justified? And if they say yes, then they are admitting that any outcome can justify any means (because they are saying that even a horrific means like human sacrifice could be justified if the outcome is good enough). But if they say no, then they are contradicting their own framework (because they said the outcome would have to be net-positive, and if the outcome is saving a million, it would be net-positive).

So the trap: Force them to say that human sacrifice would be justified in a case where it saves a million lives. If they say yes, then they are advocating for extremism. If they say no, then they are saying that not all net-positive outcomes justify the means (so they are admitting that their framework is not as flexible as they claim, and they are relying on some absolute rule they don't want to admit).

But note: the AC says they are not saying "any outcome justifies any action", so they would probably say that human sacrifice is not justified because it's too extreme. But then we can say: what makes it too extreme? If it's not because of the outcome (which is net-positive) then it must be because of the means (which is inherently bad). So they are implicitly relying on a deontological rule that the means must not be inherently bad, which contradicts their consequentialist framework.

Therefore, we should target the concession that they would justify the Manhattan Project (which they claim) but then ask about a more extreme case to see if they draw a line.

Also, note the AC's evidence: They cite Hurley. We can challenge whether Hurley actually supports their interpretation (they say Hurley says act-consequentialism judges wrongness by consequences, but then they say they don't use act-consequentialism for decision-making). But that's a bit complex for CX.

If they say no, then they are admitting that not all outcomes justify all means, but then we can ask: what is the line? And they might say "because slavery is inherently wrong", which contradicts their consequentialism.

So the key concession we want: To get them to say that they believe that in some cases, even very harmful means (like slavery) could be justified if the outcome is good enough. But they won't say that because they know it's extreme. So we have to be careful.

However, the AC might avoid the second one by saying "it's not a current scenario". So we'll stick to the first.

But we also have the smallpox example. We can ask about that too, but let's focus on the Manhattan Project because it's the most extreme.

So, target concessions:
- Concession 1: The AC admits that the means (nuclear weapons) in the Manhattan Project were justified by the outcome (saving lives).
- Concession 2: The AC admits that they are not claiming that the outcome in the Manhattan Project was the only outcome they considered (i.e., they did consider long-term consequences and still found it net-positive).

But note: they already said they did consider the outcome. So we don't need to get that.

Alternatively, to set up the contradiction: We want to get them to say that they believe in the possibility of justifying harmful means (which they do, by their examples). Then we can say: "So you are saying that in some cases, harmful means can be justified, which is exactly what the Negative says the resolution means." But they say they are not saying that. So we have a contradiction.

If they say yes, then they are admitting that harmful means can be justified (which is the very thing they are trying to deny is what the resolution means).

So, that's the core.

Let's formulate the questions:

[[ ## reasoning ## ]]
The AC's speech hinges on redefining the resolution to avoid extremism by claiming "can" implies possibility (not universality) and that consequentialism evaluates outcomes through practical rules. However, their own examples (Manhattan Project, smallpox eradication) directly contradict their defense: they explicitly justify horrific means (nuclear weapons, forced vaccination) as outcomes-driven. This exposes a fatal weakness: by admitting these cases as "justified," they concede the resolution's core claim ("ends can justify means") is true in specific instances. The AC's argument collapses because their interpretation isn't distinct from the "extremism" they deny—they're admitting the resolution is valid *in practice* for harmful means. 

The judge (community college, prefers clear structure) will prioritize this contradiction over philosophical nuance. We must force a concession that the AC's examples *require* the resolution to be true, making their "not extremism" claim self-defeating. Targeting the Manhattan Project is optimal: it's the most extreme example, and the AC explicitly states it was "justified" by outcomes. Asking about it exposes their position as a slippery slope trap (if nuclear weapons are justified, why not other extreme means?).

We choose **establish_contradiction** as the strategy because it directly attacks the AC's central claim of "not advocating extremism" using their own evidence. This is more effective than challenging evidence (which would require deep philosophy) or seeking ambiguous concessions. The concession "Manhattan Project means were justified by outcome" is irrefutable from their speech and will anchor the NEG's next speech.

[[ ## strategy ## ]]
establish_contradiction: Force the AC to concede that their *own example* proves the resolution is true (i.e., ends justified means in a case of horrific harm). This exposes the logical contradiction between their claim of "not advocating extremism" and their admission of justifying harmful means. The concession will be weaponized in the NEG's next speech to prove the resolution inherently enables extremism.

[[ ## target_concessions ## ]]
["In the Manhattan Project, the outcome (ending WWII and saving lives) justified the means (using nuclear weapons)."]

[[ ## completed ## ]]

dimensional_scores.cx_questioner_feedback: This is high-quality cross-examination that demonstrates sophisticated trap-setting and strategic questioning. The Negative successfully builds a four-question progression that extracts increasingly damaging concessions, culminating in getting the Affirmative to admit they justify 'horrific harm as a means to an end'—which can be powerfully used to argue the resolution enables extremism. The questioning shows excellent strategic planning and execution. The main weakness is the lack of independent evidence grounding, though this is somewhat inherent to cross-examination format. For a lay judge, the argument thread is mostly clear but could benefit from more explicit signposting about why each concession matters.

[[ ## topic ## ]]
The ends can justify the means

[[ ## cx_type ## ]]
AC-CX

[[ ## your_side ## ]]
AFF

[[ ## your_speech ## ]]
Thank you. The resolution before us is simple: The ends can justify the means. But what this resolution actually means is where the debate will be decided. I affirm this resolution under the only reasonable interpretation: Consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating whether particular means are justified by their particular outcomes in specific circumstances. This is not the same as saying any outcome justifies any action. This is the only way to understand the resolution in ordinary language, and this is the only way to understand consequentialist ethics correctly.

Let me diagnose the actual disagreement. The Negative will claim I am defending extremism. They will say I am arguing that we can justify any action if the outcome is good. But that is not what the resolution says, and that is not what I am arguing. The resolution says the ends can justify the means. The word can implies possibility, not universality. It asks whether there are any circumstances where a good outcome can be a sufficient reason to adopt a difficult action. This is a claim about how moral evaluation works, not a command to do whatever works.

To understand why this interpretation is correct, we must understand consequentialist ethics. According to philosopher Mark Hurley's distinction, consequentialist ethics has three components: what makes acts morally wrong, what procedure agents should use to make moral decisions, and what conditions justify moral sanctions. The key point is this: act-consequentialism judges wrongness by consequences. As Hurley explains, "An act is wrong if and only if it results in less good than would have resulted from some available alternative act."

This matters because it establishes the core principle: means are justified if they produce net-positive outcomes. But here is where we resolve the tension between extremism and restraint. Consequentialist ethics does not require us to calculate every outcome in every situation. In fact, as Hurley explains, consequentialists reject the act-consequentialist decision procedure because "very often the agent does not have detailed information about what the consequences would be of various acts." Instead, we use rules whose acceptance will produce the best consequences.

This is the reasonable middle ground. We evaluate means by their outcomes, but we do so through practical rules that account for human limitations. This is not a license for tyranny. This is the framework that allows us to make moral decisions in the real world.

Let me illustrate with two historical cases. First, the Manhattan Project. The development and use of nuclear weapons ended World War II faster, saving millions of lives. The means were controversial, even horrific. But the outcome was net-positive. Under consequentialist ethics, this outcome justifies the means. Not because we should casually embrace nuclear weapons, but because this case demonstrates that evaluating means by outcomes is the only ethical framework that adapts to new evidence and changing circumstances.

Second, smallpox eradication. This required mandatory vaccination programs and quarantine measures that would be condemned under deontological ethics. But the outcome was the complete eradication of a disease that killed millions. The means were justified by the outcome. Again, this is not an endorsement of unlimited government power. It is an example of how weighing outcomes over fixed rules saves lives.

Now let me address the tension directly. The Negative will cite cases like human sacrifice or slavery to claim my position is extremist. But this misreads both the resolution and my argument. The resolution asks whether the ends can justify the means. It does not ask whether every end justifies every means. The Negative's examples prove my point: we condemn slavery not because of some fixed rule, but because of the net-negative outcomes it produces. Consequentialist ethics demands we weigh all foreseeable harms and benefits.

This is the key insight: means are justified by outcomes, not by any outcome. If an outcome is net-negative, the means are not justified. This resolves the moral tension without endorsing extremism.

So let me be clear about what I am affirming and what I am not. I affirm that consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating means by outcomes. I do not affirm that any outcome justifies any action. The resolution is true under the only reasonable interpretation, and this interpretation is both philosophically sound and practically necessary for moral decision-making.

This matters because it provides the only ethical framework that adapts to new evidence and changing circumstances. Fixed rules cannot account for the complexity of human life. Only outcome evaluation can guide us through moral uncertainty.

For these reasons, I urge an affirmative ballot. The resolution is true when understood as consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating means by outcomes. This is the only way to read the resolution in ordinary language, and this is the only way to understand consequentialist ethics correctly.

The opponent is likely to attack on:
- Evidence quality (e.g., Hooker citations, historical examples)
- Logical consistency (e.g., the tension between "ends can justify the means" and avoiding extremism)
- Impact claims (e.g., whether the examples actually prove the point)

From the perspective node (AFF), we have a detailed argument structure. The core of our argument is that the resolution must be interpreted as: "Consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating whether particular means are justified by their particular outcomes in specific circumstances" (i.e., it's about possibility, not universality).

Vital premises (cannot be conceded) are the ones that are foundational to our interpretation and the resolution. These include:
1. The resolution must be interpreted as "can" (possibility) not "always" (universality).
2. Consequentialist ethics (as defined by Hooker) evaluates wrongness by consequences (act-consequentialism).
3. The examples (Manhattan Project, smallpox) are valid because they show net-positive outcomes justifying controversial means.

Acceptable concessions: 
- We might concede that in some cases (e.g., if the outcome is net-negative) the means are not justified. But note: our argument already says the resolution doesn't claim "any outcome justifies any action", so this is not a concession but a clarification.

Trap anticipation: 
- The opponent might try to set traps by asking for a specific example of a "bad end" that justifies a means (to show we are endorsing extremism) or by misrepresenting our argument as "any outcome justifies any action".
- They might ask about the accuracy of historical examples (e.g., did the Manhattan Project really save lives?).

Redirect strengths: 
- We should redirect to the Hooker definition of consequentialism (which we've built our case on) and to the examples we provided (Manhattan Project, smallpox) as clear cases of net-positive outcomes justifying means.

Defense posture: 
- We are AFF and in AC-CX, so we are the first speaker to be cross-examined. We should be assertive but not aggressive, and we must stick to our interpretation.

Let's break down:

Vital Premises (must not concede):
- The resolution is about possibility (can), not universality (always). If we concede that the resolution means "always", we lose.
- Consequentialist ethics (as per Hooker) defines moral wrongness by consequences (so the means are judged by the outcome). If we concede that consequentialism uses fixed rules, we lose.
- Our examples (Manhattan Project, smallpox) are valid because they had net-positive outcomes and were justified under consequentialism.

Acceptable Concessions (we can give up without losing):
- We can concede that there are cases where the outcome is net-negative (so the means are not justified). But note: this is already part of our argument (we say the resolution doesn't claim "any outcome justifies any action"). So this is not really a concession but a clarification. However, we might be asked to say "in the case of slavery, the outcome is net-negative so the means are not justified" — we can say that without losing because we already said that.

But note: the opponent might try to say "if you say the ends can justify the means, then slavery could be justified if the outcome was good". We must not concede that slavery could ever be justified (because we say the outcome of slavery is net-negative). So we cannot concede that slavery is ever justified. But we can say that in the case of slavery, the outcome is net-negative so it's not justified — which is consistent with our argument.

Therefore, acceptable concessions might include:
- Acknowledging that we do not claim that every outcome justifies every means (which we already said, so it's not a concession but a restatement).
- Maybe conceding that in some cases, the means might be too horrific to consider (but note: our argument says we weigh all foreseeable harms and benefits, so we would have considered the horrific nature as a harm).

But note: the opponent might try to set a trap by asking: "If the end was to save 1000 lives, would you justify torturing one person?" We must say: "Only if the outcome was net-positive (i.e., the 1000 lives saved minus the harm of torture is positive)." But we cannot say "yes, torture is always justified" — we must say it's conditional on the outcome.

So acceptable concessions might be:
- "Yes, in a hypothetical where the outcome is net-positive, the means would be justified." (But note: we are not conceding that torture would be justified in reality because we don't know the outcome, but in the hypothetical we are using for argument, we say it would be.)

However, we must be careful: we are not going to say "yes" to torture in a real-world scenario without the outcome being net-positive. But the question is about the hypothetical.

But note: the opponent might try to trick us by saying "so you are saying torture is justified if the outcome is good". We can say: "Yes, if the outcome is net-positive. But note, we must weigh all foreseeable harms and benefits, so in reality, torture would be very difficult to justify because the harm of torture is often very high and the outcome is uncertain."

So acceptable concession: "We agree that if the outcome is net-positive, then the means are justified. But we also agree that in many cases, the outcome would not be net-positive, so the means would not be justified."

Trap anticipation:

[[ ## speech_content ## ]]
Thank you. The resolution before us is simple: The ends can justify the means. But what this resolution actually means is where the debate will be decided. I affirm this resolution under the only reasonable interpretation: Consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating whether particular means are justified by their particular outcomes in specific circumstances. This is not the same as saying any outcome justifies any action. This is the only way to understand the resolution in ordinary language, and this is the only way to understand consequentialist ethics correctly.

Let me diagnose the actual disagreement. The Negative will claim I am defending extremism. They will say I am arguing that we can justify any action if the outcome is good. But that is not what the resolution says, and that is not what I am arguing. The resolution says the ends can justify the means. The word can implies possibility, not universality. It asks whether there are any circumstances where a good outcome can be a sufficient reason to adopt a difficult action. This is a claim about how moral evaluation works, not a command to do whatever works.

To understand why this interpretation is correct, we must understand consequentialist ethics. According to philosopher Mark Hurley's distinction, consequentialist ethics has three components: what makes acts morally wrong, what procedure agents should use to make moral decisions, and what conditions justify moral sanctions. The key point is this: act-consequentialism judges wrongness by consequences. As Hurley explains, "An act is wrong if and only if it results in less good than would have resulted from some available alternative act."

This matters because it establishes the core principle: means are justified if they produce net-positive outcomes. But here is where we resolve the tension between extremism and restraint. Consequentialist ethics does not require us to calculate every outcome in every situation. In fact, as Hurley explains, consequentialists reject the act-consequentialist decision procedure because "very often the agent does not have detailed information about what the consequences would be of various acts." Instead, we use rules whose acceptance will produce the best consequences.

This is the reasonable middle ground. We evaluate means by their outcomes, but we do so through practical rules that account for human limitations. This is not a license for tyranny. This is the framework that allows us to make moral decisions in the real world.

Let me illustrate with two historical cases. First, the Manhattan Project. The development and use of nuclear weapons ended World War II faster, saving millions of lives. The means were controversial, even horrific. But the outcome was net-positive. Under consequentialist ethics, this outcome justifies the means. Not because we should casually embrace nuclear weapons, but because this case demonstrates that evaluating means by outcomes is the only ethical framework that adapts to new evidence and changing circumstances.

Second, smallpox eradication. This required mandatory vaccination programs and quarantine measures that would be condemned under deontological ethics. But the outcome was the complete eradication of a disease that killed millions. The means were justified by the outcome. Again, this is not an endorsement of unlimited government power. It is an example of how weighing outcomes over fixed rules saves lives.

Now let me address the tension directly. The Negative will cite cases like human sacrifice or slavery to claim my position is extremist. But this misreads both the resolution and my argument. The resolution asks whether the ends can justify the means. It does not ask whether every end justifies every means. The Negative's examples prove my point: we condemn slavery not because of some fixed rule, but because of the net-negative outcomes it produces. Consequentialist ethics demands we weigh all foreseeable harms and benefits.

This is the key insight: means are justified by outcomes, not by any outcome. If an outcome is net-negative, the means are not justified. This resolves the moral tension without endorsing extremism.

So let me be clear about what I am affirming and what I am not. I affirm that consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating means by outcomes. I do not affirm that any outcome justifies any action. The resolution is true under the only reasonable interpretation, and this interpretation is both philosophically sound and practically necessary for moral decision-making.

This matters because it provides the only ethical framework that adapts to new evidence and changing circumstances. Fixed rules cannot account for the complexity of human life. Only outcome evaluation can guide us through moral uncertainty.

For these reasons, I urge an affirmative ballot. The resolution is true when understood as consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating means by outcomes. This is the only way to read the resolution in ordinary language, and this is the only way to understand consequentialist ethics correctly.

We are in an AC-CX (Affirmative Cross-Examination) setting. The strategy is to establish a contradiction by forcing the AC to concede that their own example (the Manhattan Project) proves the resolution is true, but also demonstrates that they are advocating for a position that could justify harmful means (extremism). The goal is to get the AC to admit that in the Manhattan Project, the horrific means were justified by the outcome, which then sets up the NEG's next speech to argue that this proves the resolution inherently enables extremism.

The AC's speech uses the Manhattan Project as an example of a case where the ends (ending WWII, saving lives) justified the means (nuclear weapons, which were horrific). The AC says: "The development and use of nuclear weapons ended World War II faster, saving millions of lives. The means were controversial, even horrific. But the outcome was net-positive. Under consequentialist ethics, this outcome justifies the means."

Our strategy is to ask a question that forces the AC to concede that in the Manhattan Project, the means (using nuclear weapons) were justified by the outcome (saving lives). But note: the AC says the means were "horrific", so we can use that to show that they are admitting the means were harmful, but they are justifying it by the outcome. This creates the contradiction: they are saying the ends justify the means (including horrific ones) and they are not advocating extremism, but the example shows they are justifying a horrific means.

However, note the AC's disclaimer: "This is not the same as saying any outcome justifies any action." But in the Manhattan Project, they are saying the horrific means were justified. So we can ask a question that forces them to admit that the means (nuclear weapons) were horrific and that the outcome justified them.

The judge is a community college judge with 3 years experience who prefers clear structure and real-world examples.

We are the NEG, and our perspective (as per the provided structure) is that the resolution leads to extremism and moral inconsistency.

Question 1 (first question) should be designed to set up the contradiction.

We want to ask: "In the Manhattan Project, the use of nuclear weapons was a horrific means that caused massive civilian casualties. But you argue that the outcome (ending WWII and saving millions of lives) justifies that horrific means. So, would you say that in this case, the horrific means were justified by the outcome?"

But note: the AC has already stated this in their speech. So we are not asking for new information, but we are asking for a concession that they have already made. However, in cross-examination, we are allowed to ask for a direct concession to make it clear for the judge.

But the strategy says: "Force the AC to concede that their *own example* proves the resolution is true (i.e., ends justified means in a case of horrific harm)."

So we are asking for a concession on the example they provided.

However, note: the AC says "the means were controversial, even horrific" and "this outcome justifies the means". So we can ask:

"Sir/Ma'am, you just stated that the Manhattan Project's use of nuclear weapons was a horrific means that was justified by the outcome of ending WWII and saving millions of lives. So, in this specific case, the horrific means were justified by the outcome?"

But we want to be precise and clear for the judge.

Also, note the strategy says: "This exposes the logical contradiction between their claim of 'not advocating extremism' and their admission of justifying harmful means."

So we are going to get the AC to admit that they are justifying a horrific means (which is the definition of extremism in this context) by the outcome.

Therefore, the question should be:

"In the Manhattan Project, you acknowledged the means (nuclear weapons) were 'horrific'. And you said that the outcome justified the means. So, would you agree that in this case, the horrific means were justified by the outcome?"

But note: the AC did say that the means were horrific and that the outcome justified the means. So they would have to say yes.

We must be careful: the AC might try to backpedal. But we are asking for a direct concession.

Let's formulate:

"Specifically, in the Manhattan Project, the use of nuclear weapons caused horrific civilian casualties. You stated that the outcome (saving millions of lives) justified the means. So, would you concede that the horrific means were justified by the outcome in this case?"

This question is clear, pointed, and uses the AC's own example.

Why does this advance the objectives?
- It forces the AC to admit that they are justifying a horrific means (which is the core of the extremism argument).
- It sets up the NEG's next speech to say: "The AC admits that in a case of horrific harm (the Manhattan Project), the means were justified by the outcome. Therefore, the resolution allows for the justification of horrific means, which is extremism."

Also, it's appropriate for the judge: clear, uses a real-world example, and is structured.

Now, note the judge's preference: clear structure and real-world examples. This question is clear and uses a real-world example (Manhattan Project) that the AC already provided.

So, the question should be:

"In the Manhattan Project, you described the use of nuclear weapons as a 'horrific' means that was justified by the outcome of ending WWII and saving millions of lives. So, would you agree that in this specific case, the horrific means were justified by the outcome?"

But to make it even more direct and to avoid any ambiguity, we can say:

"Specifically, in the Manhattan Project, the means (nuclear weapons) caused horrific civilian casualties. You stated that the outcome justified the means. Therefore, would you concede that the horrific means were justified by the outcome?"

Now, let's write the reasoning.

Reasoning:
This question forces the AC to explicitly concede that their own example (Manhattan Project) involves justifying a horrific means by the outcome. This directly contradicts their claim that they are not advocating for extremism, as justifying horrific means (like nuclear weapons in WWII) is a classic example of extremism. The concession will be used in the NEG's next speech to argue that the resolution inherently enables extremism by allowing the justification of harmful means in specific cases.

Now, the question:

"In the Manhattan Project, the means (nuclear weapons) caused horrific civilian casualties. You stated that the outcome justified the means. Therefore, would you concede that the horrific means were justified by the outcome?"

But note: the AC did not say "the horrific means were justified by the outcome" verbatim. They said: "the outcome was net-positive. Under consequentialist ethics, this outcome justifies the means." So we are paraphrasing correctly.

We can also use the exact words they used: "the outcome was net-positive. Under consequentialist ethics, this outcome justifies the means."

So the question can be:

"In the Manhattan Project, you stated that the outcome (ending WWII and saving millions) was net-positive and that this outcome justifies the means. Since the means (nuclear weapons) were horrific, would you agree that in this case, the horrific means were justified by the outcome?"

But to be concise and direct, we'll go with:

"In the Manhattan Project, you described the means as 'horrific' and stated that the outcome justified the means. So, would you concede that in this specific case, the horrific means were justified by the outcome?"

This is clear and uses the AC's own words.

The questioning shows excellent strategic planning and execution. The main weakness is the lack of independent evidence grounding, though this is somewhat inherent to cross-examination format. The ends can justify the means

[[ ## cx_type ## ]]
AC-CX

[[ ## your_speech ## ]]
Thank you. The resolution before us is simple: The ends can justify the means. But what this resolution actually means is where the debate will be decided. I affirm this resolution under the only reasonable interpretation: Consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating whether particular means are justified by their particular outcomes in specific circumstances. This is not the same as saying any outcome justifies any action. This is the only way to understand the resolution in ordinary language, and this is the only way to understand consequentialist ethics correctly.

Let me diagnose the actual disagreement. The Negative will claim I am defending extremism. They will say I am arguing that we can justify any action if the outcome is good. But that is not what the resolution says, and that is not what I am arguing. The resolution says the ends can justify the means. The word can implies possibility, not universality. It asks whether there are any circumstances where a good outcome can be a sufficient reason to adopt a difficult action. This is a claim about how moral evaluation works, not a command to do whatever works.

To understand why this interpretation is correct, we must understand consequentialist ethics. According to philosopher Mark Hurley's distinction, consequentialist ethics has three components: what makes acts morally wrong, what procedure agents should use to make moral decisions, and what conditions justify moral sanctions. The key point is this: act-consequentialism judges wrongness by consequences. As Hurley explains, "An act is wrong if and only if it results in less good than would have resulted from some available alternative act."

This matters because it establishes the core principle: means are justified if they produce net-positive outcomes. But here is where we resolve the tension between extremism and restraint. Consequentialist ethics does not require us to calculate every outcome in every situation. In fact, as Hurley explains, consequentialists reject the act-consequentialist decision procedure because "very often the agent does not have detailed information about what the consequences would be of various acts." Instead, we use rules whose acceptance will produce the best consequences.

This is the reasonable middle ground. We evaluate means by their outcomes, but we do so through practical rules that account for human limitations. This is not a license for tyranny. This is the framework that allows us to make moral decisions in the real world.

Let me illustrate with two historical cases. First, the Manhattan Project. The development and use of nuclear weapons ended World War II faster, saving millions of lives. The means were controversial, even horrific. But the outcome was net-positive. Under consequentialist ethics, this outcome justifies the means. Not because we should casually embrace nuclear weapons, but because this case demonstrates that evaluating means by outcomes is the only ethical framework that adapts to new evidence and changing circumstances.

Second, smallpox eradication. This required mandatory vaccination programs and quarantine measures that would be condemned under deontological ethics. But the outcome was the complete eradication of a disease that killed millions. The means were justified by the outcome. Again, this is not an endorsement of unlimited government power. It is an example of how weighing outcomes over fixed rules saves lives.

Now let me address the tension directly. The Negative will cite cases like human sacrifice or slavery to claim my position is extremist. But this misreads both the resolution and my argument. The resolution asks whether the ends can justify the means. It does not ask whether every end justifies every means. The Negative's examples prove my point: we condemn slavery not because of some fixed rule, but because of the net-negative outcomes it produces. Consequentialist ethics demands we weigh all foreseeable harms and benefits.

This is the key insight: means are justified by outcomes, not by any outcome. If an outcome is net-negative, the means are not justified. This resolves the moral tension without endorsing extremism.

So let me be clear about what I am affirming and what I am not. I affirm that consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating means by outcomes. I do not affirm that any outcome justifies any action. The resolution is true under the only reasonable interpretation, and this interpretation is both philosophically sound and practically necessary for moral decision-making.

This matters because it provides the only ethical framework that adapts to new evidence and changing circumstances. Fixed rules cannot account for the complexity of human life. Only outcome evaluation can guide us through moral uncertainty.

For these reasons, I urge an affirmative ballot. The resolution is true when understood as consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating means by outcomes. This is the only way to read the resolution in ordinary language, and this is the only way to understand consequentialist ethics correctly.

We are given a cross-examination question from the Negative side in an AC-CX (Affirmative Cross-Examination) setting. The question is: "In the Manhattan Project, you described the means as 'horrific' and stated the outcome justified the means. So, would you concede that in this specific case, the horrific means were justified by the outcome?"

The Affirmative perspective (from the perspective_node) is grounded in consequentialist ethics as defined by Hooker, with the key point being that the resolution means "the ends can justify the means" in the sense that there are circumstances where a net-positive outcome justifies a particular means. The Affirmative must avoid conceding that any means are justified by any outcome (which would be the extreme interpretation the Negative is trying to pin them to) and instead must clarify that the justification is conditional on the outcome being net-positive.

The question is a trap: it says the Affirmative described the means as "horrific" and then asks if they would concede the horrific means were justified by the outcome. The Affirmative must answer "yes" to the specific case (because they did argue that in the Manhattan Project, the outcome justified the means) but must immediately clarify the conditionality (i.e., it's not that any horrific means are justified, but only those that lead to a net-positive outcome).

However, note that the Affirmative's own speech already stated: "the outcome was net-positive. Under consequentialist ethics, this outcome justifies the means." So they are committed to saying that in this specific case, the means were justified. But they must not let the Negative interpret this as a general license.

The judge is a community college judge who prefers clear structure and real-world examples, so the answer should be clear and directly address the question without being overly complex.

Strategic considerations:
1. Do not avoid the question: Answer "yes" to the specific case (because the Affirmative's own speech said so) but immediately add the necessary qualification.
2. Avoid the concession that the means were justified without condition: The Affirmative must stress that the justification was because of the net-positive outcome (which they have already established in their speech).
3. Redirect to the strength: The Affirmative's strength is that they are not endorsing any means for any outcome, but only when the outcome is net-positive. So the answer should reinforce that.

The answer should be: "Yes, I would concede that in the specific case of the Manhattan Project, the horrific means (the development and use of nuclear weapons) were justified by the outcome (ending World War II and saving millions of lives), because the outcome was net-positive. But I must emphasize that this does not mean that any horrific means are justified by any outcome. The justification is strictly conditional on the outcome being net-positive, as I explained in my speech."

But note: the question says "the horrific means were justified by the outcome" — the Affirmative must agree to the specific case (as they did in their speech) but must not allow the Negative to take it as a general principle.

Also, the Affirmative's speech already used the Manhattan Project as an example to show that a net-positive outcome justifies the means. So they are committed to that.

Now, looking at the perspective_node, the key belief is belief_12 (and related arguments) which states: "The 'inherent justification' of means under consequentialism depends on accurate prediction of outcomes." And the evidence (Hooker) says that an act is wrong if it results in less good than an alternative. So the justification is conditional on the outcome.

Therefore, the answer must be a "yes" to the specific case but with the condition.

Let's craft the answer:

Answer: "Yes, I would concede that in the Manhattan Project, the horrific means were justified by the outcome because the outcome was net-positive. But this is not a blanket endorsement of horrific means; it's a specific application of consequentialist ethics where the outcome (ending the war and saving lives) outweighed the harms. As I explained in my speech, consequentialism requires that we evaluate means by outcomes, but only when the outcome is net-positive. This is the only way to avoid extremism, as I've argued."

Now, the reasoning should explain why this answer is strategic.

Reasoning:
- The question is a direct challenge to the Affirmative's own example. The Affirmative must not dodge it, so they must say "yes" to the specific case they've already argued.
- However, the Negative is trying to pin the Affirmative to an extremist interpretation. The Affirmative must immediately clarify the conditionality to prevent that misinterpretation.
- By saying "yes" and then immediately adding the condition, the Affirmative both concedes the point (which they must, because they've already argued it) and redirects to their core strength (the conditional nature of the justification).
- This answer is clear, structured, and uses the same language as the Affirmative's speech (net-positive outcome), which aligns with the judge's preference for clarity and real-world examples.

Note: The Affirmative's speech used the phrase "net-positive outcome", so the answer should use that term to maintain consistency.

Therefore, the answer will be:
"Yes, I would concede that in the Manhattan Project, the horrific means were justified by the outcome because the outcome was net-positive. But this does not mean that any horrific means are justified by any outcome; it only means that when an outcome is net-positive, the means that produced it are justified. This is the conditional interpretation I've been arguing for throughout my speech."

But note: the question says "the horrific means were justified by the outcome" — the Affirmative must say "yes" to that phrasing, but they must immediately add the condition.

We'll write the answer as above.

Now, the output must be in the exact structure.

</think>

Q1: In the Manhattan Project, you described the means as "horrific" and stated the outcome justified the means. So, would you concede that in this specific case, the horrific means were justified by the outcome?
A1: Yes, I would concede that in the Manhattan Project, the horrific means were justified by the outcome because the outcome was net-positive—ending World War II and saving millions of lives. But this is not a license for tyranny. As I explained in my speech, justification only applies when the outcome is net-positive, as required by consequentialist ethics. [[ ## speech_content ## ]]
Thank you. The resolution before us is simple: The ends can justify the means. But what this resolution actually means is where the debate will be decided. I affirm this resolution under the only reasonable interpretation: Consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating whether particular means are justified by their particular outcomes in specific circumstances. This is not the same as saying any outcome justifies any action. This is the only way to understand the resolution in ordinary language, and this is the only way to understand consequentialist ethics correctly.

Let me diagnose the actual disagreement. The Negative will claim I am defending extremism. They will say I am arguing that we can justify any action if the outcome is good. But that is not what the resolution says, and that is not what I am arguing. The resolution says the ends can justify the means. The word can implies possibility, not universality. It asks whether there are any circumstances where a good outcome can be a sufficient reason to adopt a difficult action. This is a claim about how moral evaluation works, not a command to do whatever works.

To understand why this interpretation is correct, we must understand consequentialist ethics. According to philosopher Mark Hurley's distinction, consequentialist ethics has three components: what makes acts morally wrong, what procedure agents should use to make moral decisions, and what conditions justify moral sanctions. The key point is this: act-consequentialism judges wrongness by consequences. As Hurley explains, "An act is wrong if and only if it results in less good than would have resulted from some available alternative act."

This matters because it establishes the core principle: means are justified if they produce net-positive outcomes. But here is where we resolve the tension between extremism and restraint. Consequentialist ethics does not require us to calculate every outcome in every situation. In fact, as Hurley explains, consequentialists reject the act-consequentialist decision procedure because "very often the agent does not have detailed information about what the consequences would be of various acts." Instead, we use rules whose acceptance will produce the best consequences.

This is the reasonable middle ground. We evaluate means by their outcomes, but we do so through practical rules that account for human limitations. This is not a license for tyranny. This is the framework that allows us to make moral decisions in the real world.

Let me illustrate with two historical cases. First, the Manhattan Project. The development and use of nuclear weapons ended World War II faster, saving millions of lives. The means were controversial, even horrific. But the outcome was net-positive. Under consequentialist ethics, this outcome justifies the means. Not because we should casually embrace nuclear weapons, but because this case demonstrates that evaluating means by outcomes is the only ethical framework that adapts to new evidence and changing circumstances.

Second, smallpox eradication. This required mandatory vaccination programs and quarantine measures that would be condemned under deontological ethics. But the outcome was the complete eradication of a disease that killed millions. The means were justified by the outcome. Again, this is not an endorsement of unlimited government power. It is an example of how weighing outcomes over fixed rules saves lives.

Now let me address the tension directly. The Negative will cite cases like human sacrifice or slavery to claim my position is extremist. But this misreads both the resolution and my argument. The resolution asks whether the ends can justify the means. It does not ask whether every end justifies every means. The Negative's examples prove my point: we condemn slavery not because of some fixed rule, but because of the net-negative outcomes it produces. Consequentialist ethics demands we weigh all foreseeable harms and benefits.

This is the key insight: means are justified by outcomes, not by any outcome. If an outcome is net-negative, the means are not justified. This resolves the moral tension without endorsing extremism.

So let me be clear about what I am affirming and what I am not. I affirm that consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating means by outcomes. I do not affirm that any outcome justifies any action. The resolution is true under the only reasonable interpretation, and this interpretation is both philosophically sound and practically necessary for moral decision-making.

This matters because it provides the only ethical framework that adapts to new evidence and changing circumstances. Fixed rules cannot account for the complexity of human life. Only outcome evaluation can guide us through moral uncertainty.

For these reasons, I urge an affirmative ballot. The resolution is true when understood as consequentialist ethics necessitates evaluating means by outcomes. This is the only way to read the resolution in ordinary language, and this is the only way to understand consequentialist ethics correctly.

The strategy is to establish a contradiction by forcing the AC to concede that their own example (Manhattan Project) proves the resolution is true, which then exposes the contradiction between their claim of "not advocating extremism" and their admission of justifying harmful means.

The AC's speech (speech_content) and their first answer (A1) have already been provided. In A1, the AC conceded that in the Manhattan Project, the horrific means were justified by the outcome, but clarified that this is only for net-positive outcomes and not a license for tyranny.

Our goal is to create a question that:
1. Advances the CX objective: force the AC to concede that their example (Manhattan Project) is a case where the resolution holds, and then show that this example is one of "horrific harm" that they are justifying, thus contradicting their claim that they are not advocating extremism.

The AC has already conceded that the means were horrific and justified by the outcome. Now, we want to push further to show that by justifying horrific means (even in a case they say was net-positive) they are opening the door to extremism.

The judge is a community college judge who prefers clear structure and real-world examples.

Perspective: NEG (we are the Negative team). Our belief structure (as per perspective_node) shows that we are arguing that justifying harmful means undermines ethical governance and creates a slippery slope.

We need a question that:
- Is clear and pointed
